original_abstract: This review explores the self-assembly of discrete [MₙL₂ₙ] coordination cages—where n ranges from 2 to 4—formed by the combination of square-planar metal cations and "banana-shaped" bis-monodentate ligands. We examine the design principles and host–guest chemistry of a diverse array of architectures, spanning from simple globular structures to more complex topologies, including intertwined knots and interpenetrated dimeric systems. Particular emphasis is placed on how ligand geometry, structural flexibility, and external stimuli—such as light and redox chemistry—can be used to modulate cavity size and dictate guest encapsulation. Examples of allosteric binding, cooperative guest uptake, and the development of functional, switchable molecular flasks are discussed.
humanized_abstract: Self-assembly of discrete [MₙL₂ₙ] coordination cages (n=2–4) made from square-planar metals centre coordinated by flexible, ‘banana-shaped’ bis-monodentate ligands is reviewed. Several cages with diverse host–guest properties ranging from basic, simple, globular structures to more complex analogues, such as knots, are described. Details on how the properties of the ligands and additional stimuli, like light or redox reactions, could influence the volume of the cavity and the uptake properties towards specific guests are highlighted, focusing on allosteric binding and cooperative recognitions, as well as the creation of molecular switchable ‘flasks’.

document.output: Self-assembly of discrete [MₙL₂ₙ] coordination cages (n=2–4) made from square-planar metals centre coordinated by flexible, ‘banana-shaped’ bis-monodentate ligands is reviewed. Several cages with diverse host–guest properties ranging from basic, simple, globular structures to more complex analogues, such as knots, are described.
